coredata.dc:title: Nanozyme-catalyzed oxygen self-supplying mitochondria-targeting nanoplatform for precise photodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Baoxuan
Xie, Jun
Wang, Peng
Tian, Jia
Zhang, Weian
coredata.dc:description: Local hypoxia in tumors poses significant challenges to the effectiveness of oxygen-dependent photodynamic therapy (PDT). Herein, a versatile mitochondrial targeting and pH-responsive nanozyme platform (noted as Mito CPs-p) is developed for solving local hypoxia in tumors and enhancing the PDT efficiency. Mito CPs-p, which consists of carbon-dot-supported atomically dispersed platinum (CPs) with further surface modifications of triphenylphosphine (PPh3) and pH-responsive porphyrin polymers. The PDT of Mito CPs-p was activated in acidic tumor microenvironment and exposed platinum for catalyzing endogenous H2O2 decomposition to produce O2 at tumor sites, due to the hydrophobic-hydrophilic transition of pH-responsive porphyrin-containing polymers in Mito CPs-p from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, which can markedly enhance PDT efficiency. Besides, the rational integration of mitochondria-targeting will improve the bioavailability of reactive oxygen species and further strengthen the PDT efficiency. In vitro results indicated that Mito CPs-p has exhibited excellent mitochondrial targeting capability and the ROS yield of Mito CPs-p was dramatically amplified. In vivo data further revealed that the Mito CPs-p displayed superb therapeutic efficacy. Hence, the Mito CPs-p provides new insights for efficient PDT in hypoxia solid tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porphyrin
Photodynamic therapy (PDT)
Hypoxia tumors
Mitochondria-targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cellular distribution and cytotoxicity of mito CPs-p
Penetration and tumor cell killing in multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTSs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of CPs synthesis; (b) The TEM image of CPs; (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum with different exiting wavelength of CPs; (d) Hydrodynamic diameter of CPs; (e) The rate changes of the hydrogen peroxide decomposition and oxygen production catalyzed by CPs under certain time intervals.

(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of CPs, POD and Mito CPs-p. (c) The rate changes of H2O2 decomposition and oxygen production catalyzed by Mito CPs-p under certain time intervals; TEM images of Mito CPs-p in (d) pH = 7.4, and (e) pH = 5.4 PBS buffer solution; (f) Changes in the zeta potential of Mito CPs-p in different pH values, (g) the hydrodynamic size curves of Mito CPs-p in different pH values, (h) the variation of fluorescence intensity of Mito CPs-p (E x = 420 nm), (i) the maximum absorbance variation of DPBF (I429) against irradiation time, with Mito CPs-p in buffer solution with different pH values.

(a) CLSM images of cellular uptake for Mito CPs-p and Mito CPs-tB by 4T1 cells at different time intervals (4 h and 24 h); (b) CLSM images using the fluorescent probe DCFH-DA (green) to measure the intracellular ROS levels of 4T1 cells; (c) The CLSM images of 4T1 cells of mitochondria targeting in Mito CPs-p treatment. 4T1 cytotoxicity histogram of Mito CPs-p, Mito CPs-tB, Mito Cs-p, and CPs-p (d) in the dark and (e) with 650 nm laser light irradiation. (f) The dark toxicity and (g) phototoxicity of Mito CPs-p and POD in normoxia. (h) The dark toxicity and (i) phototoxicity of Mito CPs-p and POD and in hypoxia. Calculated as mean ± SD (n = 5), * p > 0.05, **p < 0.05 and ***p < 0.001.

CLSM images showing in vitro penetration of (a) Mito CPs-tB and (b) Mito CPs-p in 4T1 multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTSs). The surface of the MCTSs was defined as 0 μm. Scale bar = 50 μm. (c) Drug metabolism and distribution in tumor-bearing mice at different time periods after administration (1 h, 3 h, 17 h, 24 h), (d) Drug distribution in various organs and tissues in mice at 24 h, I) tumor, II) heart, III) liver, IV) spleen, V) lung, VI) kidney.

In vivo anti-tumor studies. (a) Schematic demonstration of the treatment process of in vivo fluorescence imaging experiment. (b) Changes in body weight of mice in different treatment groups (n = 4). (c) Changes in tumor volume of mice in different treatment groups (n = 4). (d) Average tumors weight on the 14th day after various treatments. (e) Photos of tumors dissected from mice receiving different treatment groups. Data calculated as mean ± SD (n = 4), * p > 0.05, **p < 0.05 and *** p < 0.001.

a) Schematic diagram of the preparation of Mito CPs-p nanoplatform. b) Schematic diagram of Mito CPs-p nanoplatform delivery system. (I) Blood circulation, (II) EPR effect, (III) Mito CPs-p nanoplatform dissociate in the acidic environment of tumor to form carbon dot-based polymers with smaller particle diameters to improve tumor penetration and to activate PDT. And after Mito CPs-p nanoplatform were internalized by tumor cells, oxygen self-supplying mitochondria-targeting photodynamic therapy.

head.ce:title: Nanozyme-catalyzed oxygen self-supplying mitochondria-targeting nanoplatform for precise photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Energy Materials and Chemistry, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy Materials and Chemistry, Inner Mongolia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Energy Materials and Chemistry, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Energy Materials and Chemistry, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy Materials and Chemistry, Inner Mongolia University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Local hypoxia in tumors poses significant challenges to the effectiveness of oxygen-dependent photodynamic therapy (PDT). Herein, a versatile mitochondrial targeting and pH-responsive nanozyme platform (noted as Mito CPs-p) is developed for solving local hypoxia in tumors and enhancing the PDT efficiency. Mito CPs-p, which consists of carbon-dot-supported atomically dispersed platinum (CPs) with further surface modifications of triphenylphosphine (PPh3) and pH-responsive porphyrin polymers. The PDT of Mito CPs-p was activated in acidic tumor microenvironment and exposed platinum for catalyzing endogenous H2O2 decomposition to produce O2 at tumor sites, due to the hydrophobic-hydrophilic transition of pH-responsive porphyrin-containing polymers in Mito CPs-p from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, which can markedly enhance PDT efficiency. Besides, the rational integration of mitochondria-targeting will improve the bioavailability of reactive oxygen species and further strengthen the PDT efficiency. In vitro results indicated that Mito CPs-p has exhibited excellent mitochondrial targeting capability and the ROS yield of Mito CPs-p was dramatically amplified. In vivo data further revealed that the Mito CPs-p displayed superb therapeutic efficacy. Hence, the Mito CPs-p provides new insights for efficient PDT in hypoxia solid tumors.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT), as a burgeoning noninvasive treatment modality, irreversibly damages the pathological cells and tissues through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the oxygen redox processes of photosensitizers upon irradiation with appropriate wavelength light [1–4]. However, the therapeutic effects of PDT are seriously limited due to the small diffusion radius (< 0.02 μm) and short lifetime (< 0.04 μs) of the generated ROS [5–8]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that the evolution of organelle-targeted photosensitizers is an effective strategy to strengthen therapeutic efficiency. The mitochondria, as vital organelles for cells, are the primary source of ROS production [9,10]. Increased ROS levels in mitochondria will bring about serious mitochondrial dysfunction. Furthermore, mitochondria mediate multiple apoptosis pathways and play a crucial role in cell death [11–15]. Therefore, mitochondria-targeted PDT has emerged as an efficient treatment method for eradicating malignant tumor cells.

Besides, tumor hypoxia caused by the unbalance between oxygen provision and consumption is a significant indicator of cancer progression [16,17]. As solid tumors grow rapidly, the incomplete establishment and insufficient function of vascular system cannot meet the tumor tissue's oxygen demands, resulting in systemic or local hypoxia and acidic environment [18–23]. The aberrant metabolism of cancer cells in solid tumors continuously generates a rising concentration of endogenous acidic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which may in turn enhance the neoplastic behaviors of tumor cells and their capacity to injure and penetrate host tissues [24–26]. Besides, this matter of typically hypoxic tumor microenvironment (TME) has been a top bottleneck for aerobic photodynamic therapy (PDT) [27–33]. Therefore, it is of the essence to develop a nanoplatform which can not only consume excess hydrogen peroxide in TME but also relieve the hypoxic TME to enhance PDT.

Here, we designed an all-in-one nanoplatform with mitochondrial targeting, oxygen-generation and pH-responsiveness for highly efficient and activated photodynamic therapy of hypoxic cancers. This versatile nanoplatform (denoted as Mito CPs-p) is constructed by means of the esterification reaction between the amino groups on the Pt-dispersed carbon dots (CDs) surface and the N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS)-activated carboxyl group on P(TPP-co-DPA)-b-(NHS-co-OEGMA) (POD) ( Scheme 1 a). When reaching the tumor tissue and exposing to the slightly acidic tumor microenvironment (TME), the Mito CPs-p nanoplatform would undergo rapid structure dissociation owing to the protonation of POD, inducing its pH-responsive hydrophobic-to-hydrophilic transition, meanwhile, PDT is activated. Importantly, the loaded Pt-dispersed CDs can continuously catalyze endogenous H2O2 in TME into dissolved oxygen, further improving the therapeutic efficiencies of oxygen-dependent PDT (Scheme 1 b). We believe that this work will supply a novel strategy to develop an intelligent photosensitizer for enhanced hypoxic PDT.

Platinum-loaded carbon dots (CPs) were prepared according to the previous work ( Fig. 1 a) [34,35]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that CPs presented a spherical morphology with a uniform particle size distribution (Fig. 1 b). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Fig. 1 d) demonstrated that the size of the as-prepared CPs ranged from 4.9 to 7.4 nm, with an average size of 5.9 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CPs (Fig. 1 c) exhibited two distinct peaks at 250 and 365 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the aromatic ring structure and n-π* transition of CO, respectively. The as-prepared CPs exhibited typical excitation-dependent photoluminescent properties. With increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 440 nm, the emission peak red shifted from 455 to 483 nm. The maximum photoluminescent intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. As shown in Fig. S1, CPs demonstrated an obvious blue fluorescence under 365 nm light. The platinum content of CPs was determined to be 0.48 wt% using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). In order to confirm its catalytic activity of CPs, the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was used as a model reaction, and the generated O2 was monitored in the H2O2 solution with or without CPs. The concentration of produced-oxygen was detected at regular interval. From Fig. 1 e, after 4 h, the rate of O2 change (
ρ
O 2
) reached 1.86 in the CPs solution, higher than 2.4 times as many as that of the aqueous solution without CPs (0.78), which verified the catalytic capability of CPs in the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.

The amphiphilic block copolymers of POD (with pH-responsiveness) and POtB (without pH-responsiveness) were synthesized via RAFT polymerization. The details of amphiphilic block copolymers are demonstrated in Table S1. The degree of polymerization (DP) for TPPC6MA (DP = 1) and DPAEMA (DP = 25) in the POD block copolymer, was calculated by comparing the area of peaks at δ = 3.38 ppm (protons of OEGMA units) to that at − 2.78 ppm (protons of TPPC6MA units, Fig. S3) and 2.98 ppm (protons of DPAEMA units) (Fig. S5). The DP of POtB was also calculated through the similar method (Fig. S6). Next, the gel permeation chromatography (GPC) curves of these polymers were shown in Fig. S7, and it could be seen that the traces of POD and POtB shifted to the higher molecular weight region in comparison with that of the P(OEGMA16-co-NHS1) copolymer. In addition, the synthesized polymers exhibited a relatively narrow polydispersity index (PDI) (Table S1), revealing that the RAFT polymerization was well-controlled.

The pH-responsive behavior of POD was confirmed by exposing them to aqueous solution at different pH (pH = 5.4, 6.4 and 7.4). From the zeta potential analysis (Fig. S8), it was found that POD showed obvious protonated tendency as the pH value decreasing. With pH = 5.4, the mean zeta potential reached up to 3.3 mV. In addition, the hydrodynamic size of POD micelles changed from 178 nm (PDI = 0.15) at pH 7.4 to 130 nm (PDI = 0.22) at pH 5.4 exhibiting a pH dependent manner, as determined by DLS (Fig. S9) and TEM (Fig. S10).

Next, the final sample, Mito CPs-p, was synthesized through the EDC/NHS catalyzed coupling reaction. The FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 2 a) of Mito CPs-p was similar to that of POD, where the introduced secondary amino group showed an evident stretching vibration absorption at 3400–3100 cm−1. Additionally, a small absorption peak at 1645 cm−1 indicated the CO vibration absorption of the carbon dot. Next, as investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy and shown the normalized spectra in Fig. 2 b, the Mito CPs-p dispersed in PBS showed typical absorbance peaks of TPP and CPs at 420 nm and 275 nm, respectively. These findings ensured the successful linkage of POD to CPs. Besides, catalytic activity of CPs in Mito CPs-p remained excellent performance (Fig. 2 c). The TEM image of Mito CPs-p revealed different morphology in aqueous media with different pH values. Under pH = 7.4, Mito CPs-p displayed dense spherical morphology with a diameter of 100 nm (Fig. 2 d). In contrast, under pH = 5.4, Mito CPs-p exhibited loose spherical morphology with numerous black dots. (Fig. 2 e). The Mito CPs-p were positively charged (Fig. 2 f) with a hydrodynamic diameter of 214.7 nm (Fig. 2 g) at pH = 7.4, where the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2 h) and the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) of TPP units in Mito CPs-p were largely quenched due to their close proximity to one another (Fig. 2 i). The detailed variation of hydrodynamic size and polydispersity index of Mito CPs-p in different pH conditions was displayed in Table S2 . As the pH gradually decreasing to pH = 5.4, the hydrodynamic diameter of Mito CPs-p changed to 1025.4 nm, in the meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity and the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) of TPP units significantly enhanced. All these results demonstrate the pH-responsive property of Mito CPs-p, enabling specific activation at the tumor microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular distribution and cytotoxicity of mito CPs-p
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of using Mito CPs-p and Mito CPs-tB for pH-activated and NIR-triggered PDT, we incubated 4T1 cells with Mito CPs-p and Mito CPs-tB. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, more intense fluorescence signal of TPP units was observed over time in 4T1 cells treated with Mito CPs-p and Mito CPs-tB. Notably, the signal intensity was higher in cells treated with Mito CPs-p compared to Mito CPs-tB. These results indicate that Mito CPs-p efficiently internalize into tumor cells and can be activated after internalization. The intracellular 1O2 generation in 4T1 cells treated with Mito Cs-p, Mito CPs-tB and Mito CPs-p nanoparticles was investigated by CLSM using DCFH-DA as the intracellular probe. In Fig. 3 b, dim green fluorescence was observed in 4T1 cells treated with Mito Cs-p or Mito CPs-tB nanoparticles after light irradiation by a 650 nm laser (0.12 W cm−2) for 10 min. It is worth noting that 4T1 cells incubated with Mito CPs-p nanoparticles exhibited bright green fluorescence, indicating their excellent capacity to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). Additionally, the fluorescence of TPP units (red) colocalized with the fluorescence from mitochondria (green) in cells incubated with Mito CPs-p, demonstrating efficient targeting of mitochondria by Mito CPs-p (Fig. 3 c).

To verify the feasibility of using Mito CPs-p for efficient pH-activated PDT under hypoxia condition, we conducted a series of cytotoxicity experiments. Firstly, we incubated 4T1 cells with the final sample Mito CPs-p, as well as control samples including Mito CPs-tB (without pH-responsive properties), CPs-p (without mitochondrial targeting), Mito Cs-p (without catalytic function for H2O2 decomposition). No obvious cytotoxicity to 4T1 cells was observed even at high concentrations of Mito CPs-tB, Mito Cs-p, Mito CPs-p and CPs-p in dark (Fig. 3 d), indicating their good biocompatibility under both normoxic (Fig. 3 f) and hypoxic conditions (Fig. 3 h). Comparing with the cells treated with Mito CPs-tB, Mito Cs-p, and CPs-p under light irradiation, the significantly lower cell viability was observed in cells treated with Mito CPs-p (Fig. 3 e). Furthermore, we evaluated the effect of oxygen availability on PDT efficiency. Regardless of whether the cells were cultured in normoxia (Fig. 3 g) or hypoxic environment (Fig. 3 i), cells incubated with Mito CPs-p showed more desirable therapeutic effects compared to those treated with Mito Cs-p under NIR irradiation. These suggests that the presence of Pt in Mito CPs-p enables the production of O2, thereby enhancing the efficacy of photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration and tumor cell killing in multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTSs)
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the acidic tumor microenvironment, we speculated that Mito CPs-p would dissociate and accumulate in the tumor microenvironment, leading to more effective intratumoral penetration ( Fig. 4 a). To investigate the penetration behaviors of different formulations, we utilized 3D multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTSs) as an in vitro model. After incubation with Mito CPs-p, those MCTSs showed strong TPP fluorescence dispersed throughout the entire spheroids. By contrast, MCTSs treated with Mito CPs-tB exhibited TPP fluorescence mostly confined to the boundary regions of the spheroids, indicating limited penetration ability (Fig. 4 b). These results suggest Mito CPs-p with pH-responsiveness show much better interstitial diffusion capability within 3D MCTSs compared to Mito CPs-tB without pH-responsiveness.

In order to confirm tumor accumulation and the metabolic distribution of Mito CPs-p in the body, we utilized in vivo bioimaging system for fluorescence imaging against tumor-bearing mice. As time went by, further high accumulation of Mito CPs-p was observed via the increased fluorescence intensity at the tumor site, which reached a maximum at 24 h (Fig. 4 c). The tumor accumulation effect benefited from the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect for the suitable size of Mito CPs-p in tumor tissue. Meanwhile, ex-vivo fluorescence images showed a strong fluorescent signal specifically localized in the tumor of mice injected with Mito CPs-p (Fig. 4 d).

Encouraged by the excellent in vitro therapeutic effect, we conducted in vivo studies using 4T1-tumor-bearing mice ( Fig. 5 a). 4T1-tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 5 groups for different treatments: a) PBS + Light; b) Mito CPs-p + Dark; c) Mito CPs-tB + Light; d) Mito Cs-p + Light; e) Mito CPs-p + Light. The doses of TPP units in different treatments were maintained at 10 mg kg−1. The body weight and tumor volume of all the mice were recorded by 2 days of the interval during the 14-d treatment period. No obvious difference in body weight was showed among the five groups (Fig. 5 b). In terms of tumor growth profiles (Fig. 5 c), tumor weight (Fig. 5 d) and tumor photograph (Fig. 5 e), the PBS with light irradiation (PBS + Light) group and Mito CPs-p without light irradiation (Mito CPs-p + Dark) group showed no distinct effect on tumor suppression. Mito CPs-tB with light irradiation (Mito CPs-tB + Light) group displayed slight antitumor effect. However, tumors treated by Mito Cs-p with light irradiation (Mito Cs-p + Light group) grew slower compared to the Mito CPs-tB + Light group, which was attributed to the activated PDT. In sharp contrast, Mito CPs-p with light irradiation (Mito CPs-p + Light group) demonstrated the strongest tumor eradication effect, indicating that the generated oxygen from the CAT-mimic catalysis by Mito CPs-p could alleviate the hypoxia without light irradiation PDT.

Additionally, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining on major organs and tumor tissue was performed to evaluate the systematic toxicity and antitumor effects after different treatments. As presented in Fig. S11, none of the formulations induced significant inflammatory lesion or side effects towards major organs. However, remarkable damage in the tumor tissues was detected in mice treated with Mito CPs-p upon 650 nm light irradiation (Fig. S12a). It is known that hypoxia-inducible factor-1 alpha (HIF-1α) is overexpressed under hypoxic conditions in solid tumors. Thus, we further investigated the expression of HIF-1α proteins in tumors from 4T1 xenograft mice. Compared with the control group, Mito CPs-p can significantly alleviate the hypoxic microenvironment at the tumor site (Fig. S12b). These results further demonstrated that Mito CPs-p exhibited negligible systematic toxicity to normal organs and excellent antitumor capacity in the presence of NIR light.

In conclusion, we have successfully constructed a multifunctional nanoplatform for efficient acid-activatable photodynamic therapy against hypoxic tumor. Experimental evidences from in vitro studies presented satisfactory biocompatibility of Mito CPs-p, allowing its further for in vitro and in vivo application. In vitro cytotoxicity studies verified that, Mito CPs-p nanoplatform exhibited the enhanced photodynamic therapeutic effects due to oxygen generation by catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2, no matter under normoxia or hypoxic condition. In vivo studies confirmed that after being accumulated within tumor sites, Mito CPs-p nanoplatform can significantly relieve tumor hypoxia and promote the effect of photodynamic therapy. This work offers new insight into the design of transformable Mito CPs-p nanoplatform and demonstrates their potential in tumor targeting and antitumor therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52203009, 52333014, 21875063) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 21ZR1417800).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxmate.2024.100230.
